articles.title: Ice Clouds Found on a Giant Alien Planet
articles.summary: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope have discovered ice clouds on a big planet far away called Epsilon Indi Ab. This planet is like Jupiter but has clouds made of water ice, which is surprising because it was thought to have ammonia clouds. The telescope helped astronomers see the planet by blocking the bright light from its star. The planet is cold but warmer than Jupiter because it still has heat from when it formed. This discovery helps us learn more about planets outside our solar system and how they are different from what we expected. It is a step toward finding planets that might be like Earth in the future.

articles.title: A New Green Snake Found in China
articles.summary: Scientists have found a new type of green snake in the mountains of China. This snake is called the Huaxi Green Pitviper, and it lives in a place with lots of plants and animals. It is green to hide in the grass and has amber eyes. People thought it was another snake, but DNA tests showed it is different. The snake is named after an old Chinese philosopher, Laozi, who taught about living in harmony with nature. It is venomous, which means it can bite and hurt people, so it's important to be careful. The discovery shows that there are still many animals we don't know about in nature.

articles.title: A Charity Helps Save a River
articles.summary: A charity called Severn Rivers Trust wants to help the River Teme. From April 22 to 29, they will match all donations people give. This means if you give money, they add the same amount. The money will be used to fix the river, make the water cleaner, protect animals and plants, and stop floods. The river has problems like pollution, not enough trees, and banks that are falling apart. The charity works with farmers and others to plant trees, make ponds, and help fish like Atlantic salmon swim better. They say everyone can help by donating, and it will make a big difference for the river.

articles.title: Saving a Tiny Spider: A New Hope
articles.summary: A special project to save a very small spider called the diamond-backed spider has gotten more money from the government. This spider was thought to be gone forever, but two volunteers found it again in 2017 at Clumber Park in England. Now, the National Trust, a charity, received over £50,000 to help protect it. They are working with spider experts to learn more about how the spider lives and breeds. The spider is only known to live at Clumber Park, so scientists want to find out if it might be in other places too. They plan to do surveys in nearby forests like Sherwood Forest and Ashdown Forest. The goal is to understand what the spider needs to survive and help it grow, not just at Clumber Park but maybe in other areas. Spiders are important because they eat pests and keep nature balanced. This funding gives hope that the spider can be saved from extinction.

articles.title: A Woman Who Learned About Stars Gets a Special Plaque
articles.summary: Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin was a smart astronomer who found out that stars are mostly made of hydrogen and helium. She lived in London as a teenager and got a scholarship to study at Cambridge. Later, she worked at Harvard University and became the first woman to be a full professor there. She wrote many papers about stars and changed how scientists understand the universe. English Heritage put a blue plaque on her old home to honor her. This plaque shows where she started her journey to become a famous scientist. Her story teaches us about hard work and not giving up, especially for women in science.

articles.title: Two Drugs Help Fix Fatty Liver in Animals
articles.summary: Fatty liver disease is a common problem where too much fat builds up in the liver, which can harm the liver and heart. Scientists from the University of Barcelona found that two medicines, pemafibrate and telmisartan, can reduce liver fat in animals like rats and zebrafish. Using both drugs together worked well and might also help the heart. These drugs are already used for other health issues, so they could be safe and fast to try for fatty liver disease. The study aims to stop the disease early before it gets worse.
